questions.question: What does pandas DataFrame.describe() return?
options: Summary statistics (count, mean, std, min, max, quartiles)
questions.explanation: describe() computes summary statistics for numerical columns: count, mean, std, min, 25%, 50% (median), 75%, max. Use describe(include='all') to include categorical columns.

questions.question: What shape does this NumPy operation produce?
questions.code:
import numpy as np
a = np.zeros((3, 4))
b = np.ones((4, 5))
result = a @ b
print(result.shape)
questions.explanation: Matrix multiplication (3×4) @ (4×5) = (3×5). The inner dimensions must match (4=4), and the result has the outer dimensions. This is the fundamental rule of matrix multiplication.

questions.question: In scikit-learn, you should fit the scaler on the test set to get the most accurate normalization.
questions.explanation: NEVER fit on test data — this causes data leakage. Fit the scaler on training data only, then transform both train and test with the same parameters. Use Pipeline to prevent accidental leakage.

questions.question: The pandas method to combine two DataFrames vertically (stacking rows) is pd.___().
questions.explanation: pd.concat([df1, df2]) stacks DataFrames vertically (axis=0, default) or horizontally (axis=1). For SQL-style joins based on keys, use pd.merge() or df.join().

questions.question: This model achieves 99% training accuracy but 52% test accuracy. Diagnose:
questions.code:
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier

model = DecisionTreeClassifier()  # no max_depth
model.fit(X_train, y_train)
print('Train:', model.score(X_train, y_train))  # 0.99
print('Test:', model.score(X_test, y_test))    # 0.52
options: Overfitting — unbounded tree memorizes training data (no regularization)
Data leakage — test set contains training information
questions.explanation: An unbounded DecisionTree (no max_depth, min_samples_leaf, etc.) grows until it perfectly fits training data, memorizing noise. Fix: set max_depth=5-10, min_samples_leaf=5+, or use ensemble methods (Random Forest).

questions.question: Your regression model has R²=0.95 on training data but R²=0.3 on new data from a different time period. What's happening?

scenario.context: Predicting house prices. Trained on 2020-2022 data. Deployed on 2024 data.

options: Data drift — the relationship between features and prices changed (market shift, inflation)
Feature engineering error — zip_code encoded differently
Target leakage — a feature implicitly contains the price
Model is too complex — needs fewer parameters
questions.explanation: Data drift (concept drift): the underlying distribution changed between training and deployment. House prices in 2024 have different relationships to features than 2020-2022 (interest rates changed, market shifted). Need: regular retraining, drift detection.

questions.question: Match each visualization to its best use case:
questions.explanation: Scatter: continuous×continuous relationships. Box: distribution shape + outliers. Heatmap: matrix values (correlations, confusion matrix). Bar: categorical comparisons.

questions.question: A binary classifier has 80 true positives, 10 false positives, 15 false negatives, and 895 true negatives. What's the F1 score?
Arrange a typical ML project workflow in correct order:
questions.explanation: The ML lifecycle: Understand data (EDA) → Prepare features → Train models → Evaluate fairly → Deploy and monitor for drift. In practice, it's iterative — evaluation failures send you back to feature engineering.

questions.question: Your hiring model has 85% accuracy overall but only 60% accuracy for minority candidates. Should you deploy it?
questions.scenario: Resume screening AI. Overall accuracy 85%. Accuracy for majority group: 90%. Accuracy for minority group: 60%. Legal requirement: no disparate impact (4/5ths rule).

options.label: Deploy — 85% overall is acceptable and saves HR time
Don't deploy — disparate accuracy violates fairness requirements
Deploy with human review for minority candidates to compensate
Retrain with fairness constraints even if overall accuracy drops
options.framework: Equity — sacrifice aggregate performance for group fairness

questions.explanation: The 4/5ths rule: minority selection rate must be ≥ 80% of majority rate. 60/90 = 67% — violates this threshold. Beyond legality, deploying a biased model causes real harm. Must investigate why (biased training data, proxy features) and fix before deployment.
